nativeRange.habitat: Found across muddy estuaries, mangrove margins, and brackish tidal zones of Amazonian and Orinoco river systems, and penetrates well into freshwater rivers and streams. In the wild it rests motionless on soft substrate or conceals itself beneath debris, ambushing prey that comes within reach.

decorPreferences: thick sand or fine-gravel substrate for resting
PVC pipes, clay caves, or hollow logs as hides
overhanging flat rocks for ambush concealment
low-light conditions to encourage daytime activity
minimal flow — sluggish or near-still water preferred
tight-fitting lid (escape risk when active at night)

diet.notes: The Freshwater Lionfish is reported to rely on live prey in the vast majority of captive accounts. Staple prey items include live feeder fish (such as goldfish, rosy reds, and guppies), live earthworms, live crayfish, and live shrimp. Some individuals can be weaned gradually onto freshly killed or frozen-thawed fish and prawns offered via feeding tongs, though acceptance varies between specimens. Feeding tongs are strongly recommended regardless — the venomous dorsal and opercular spines pose a serious envenomation risk during hand-feeding. Feeding every three to five days is sufficient; the species has a slow metabolism and gains weight quickly if overfed. Prepared pellets and dried foods are generally refused.

avoidWith: all other fish (gape-limited predator; will consume anything that fits in mouth)
conspecifics (intense territorial aggression; severe bite wounds reported)
any tankmate small enough to be swallowed whole

imageCandidates.notes: Glynn Morris, 2048x1565. Whole fish shown in lateral profile — stocky, dorsally flattened toadfish body, broad flat head, wide mouth and mottled brown camouflage clearly visible; correct ID. Sharp, highest-res option. Caveat: freshly-caught specimen held on a Guyanese mudflat (out of water); no live aquarium/in-situ CC photo of this cryptic mud-dweller exists.
Glynn Morris, 511x908. Tighter crop of the same catch — subject fills frame, showing head, gill covers and mottled body detail. Correct ID. Caveat: lower resolution and out-of-water catch pose.

summary: The Freshwater Lionfish is a venomous Amazonian toadfish — family Batrachoididae — with no relation to the true lionfishes of the genus Pterois. It grows to around 45 cm, tolerates water from freshwater to brackish, and relies almost exclusively on live prey. Its venomous dorsal and gill-cover spines make it a species for experienced keepers only.
careNotes:
The name Freshwater Lionfish is a misnomer: this species belongs to Batrachoididae (toadfishes), not Scorpaenidae, and has no close relationship to true lionfishes. The common name comes from a superficial resemblance to spiny marine lionfishes. Batrachoides surinamensis is a stocky, dorsally flattened bottom-dweller and ambush predator native to Amazonian and Guyanese estuaries. Wild specimens tolerate a wide range of salinities — from near-fresh river water up to full brackish estuaries — making them genuinely euryhaline (able to live in both fresh and salt-influenced water). In captivity, maintaining a slight brackish component (SG 1.002–1.010) is widely recommended to ease the fish's salt balance regulation and support long-term health, though the species will survive in plain dechlorinated freshwater.

HANDLING WARNING: Batrachoides surinamensis possesses both hollow venomous dorsal fin spines and venomous opercular (gill-cover) spines. Envenomation causes immediate intense pain, localised tissue necrosis, and in severe cases systemic effects. Never handle bare-handed. Use thick rubber gloves, a net, or a container when moving the fish. Keep a first-aid plan available; seek medical attention for any puncture wound.

Tank requirements: A minimum 400 L tank (150 cm footprint) is needed for a single adult. The substrate should be at least 5 cm of soft sand — the fish rests on the bottom for extended periods and can partially bury itself. Provide multiple caves and overhangs (PVC pipes, clay pots, flat rocks). Lighting should be dim; the species is nocturnal and will commonly refuse food under bright illumination. Water movement should be minimal. A tight-fitting, weighted lid is essential — the fish may attempt to escape, particularly at night.

Feeding: The Freshwater Lionfish tends to be live-feeder dependent. Feeder goldfish and rosy reds are convenient but nutritionally poor as a long-term staple; rotate with live earthworms, freshwater crayfish, and live shrimp. Some individuals can be weaned onto frozen-thawed items offered via tongs over several weeks. Feed every three to five days; the species has a slow metabolism and is easily overfed. Adults can tolerate fasting for several weeks without apparent harm.

Vocalisation: Batrachoides surinamensis is a sonic toadfish; males produce a distinctive boat-whistle or fog-horn grunt using specialised swim-bladder muscles. The sound is audible outside the tank and is associated with territorial display and spawning readiness.

Note on taxonomy and trade names: The fish is sold as 'Freshwater Lionfish' and occasionally 'Freshwater Scorpionfish' in the ornamental trade, creating confusion with true lionfishes (Pterois spp., family Scorpaenidae). All three common names refer to the same species: Batrachoides surinamensis, family Batrachoididae.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been widely documented in the aquarium hobby. In the wild, toadfishes (family Batrachoididae) are bottom-spawning species in which males guard clutches of eggs deposited in sheltered cavities — under rocks or in burrows — fanning and cleaning the eggs until they hatch. Larvae are large and lecithotrophic (yolk-sac dependent) at hatching, meaning they draw nutrition from an attached yolk sac rather than requiring immediate live food. Any captive breeding attempt would likely require brackish water, a suitable spawning cave, and a compatible pair, but sexing the fish is difficult and conspecific aggression is severe enough to make supervised pairing a significant undertaking.

primary.name: FishBase — Batrachoides surinamensis
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided taxonomy (Batrachoididae, Batrachoidiformes), native range (Suriname, Brazil, Trinidad, Venezuela, Guyana, French Guiana), adult TL to 45 cm, temperature range, habitat (muddy estuaries and freshwater rivers), diet (carnivore; fish and invertebrates), and synonymy. Confirmed euryhaline, benthic, and venomous-spine classification.

additional.name: SeriouslyFish — Batrachoides surinamensis
additional.notes: Provided detailed husbandry: minimum tank 400 L, substrate needs, brackish tolerance (SG up to 1.010), live-feeder diet dependency, venomous spine envenomation risk (dorsal and opercular), nocturnal behaviour, vocalisation via swim-bladder, and single-specimen keeping requirement. Key source for careNotes.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Toadfish Profile
additional.notes: Trade publication profile; confirmed common name confusion (Freshwater Lionfish vs true Pterois lionfishes), ambush predator behaviour, feeding strategy, brackish preference, and size reaching 40+ cm. Reinforced specialist-only recommendation.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Venomous and Dangerous Aquarium Fish
additional.notes: Consulted for general venomous-spine handling guidance applicable to Batrachoididae; confirmed that opercular spines in toadfishes are venomous and that envenomation warrants medical attention. No dedicated Batrachoides surinamensis page exists on Aquarium Co-Op.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Freshwater Toadfishes in the Aquarium
additional.notes: Historical hobbyist account of Batrachoididae in captivity; confirmed vocalisation behaviour (swim-bladder sonic muscles), feeding challenges with live-only diet, and difficulty of conspecific housing. Supplementary source for breedingNotes and vocalisation detail.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been documented in the aquarium hobby. In the wild, toadfishes are bottom-spawning species in which males guard egg clutches deposited in sheltered cavities until hatching. Replicating these conditions in captivity would require brackish water, a suitable spawning cave, and a compatible pair, but conspecific aggression is severe enough to make any pairing attempt very difficult.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Not actually a lionfish (Batrachoididae toadfish family). Brackish-tolerant ambush predator reaching 40+cm with VENOMOUS DORSAL/OPERCULAR SPINES capable of severe envenomation. Single-fish species-only tank; live-feeder dependent.
